diagrams.title: Entity Integrity and Primary Key Constraints
diagrams.description: Shows two related tables (Customers and Orders) with primary key constraints highlighted. Demonstrates how each table has a primary key (PK) that uniquely identifies rows, with the Customers table showing how primary key constraints prevent duplicate and null values.
diagrams.purpose: Explains entity integrity - the concept that primary keys must contain unique, non-null values to uniquely identify each record in a table

diagrams.title: Referential Integrity with Foreign Key Constraints
diagrams.description: Illustrates the parent-child relationship between Customers and Orders tables with a foreign key constraint. Shows the referential integrity rule: the foreign key (customer_id in Orders) must reference an existing primary key value in Customers or be null.
diagrams.purpose: Explains referential integrity - ensuring that relationships between tables remain consistent, and demonstrates how foreign key constraints maintain data integrity across related tables

diagrams.title: Constraint Types and Enforcement Actions
diagrams.description: A visual matrix showing different constraint types (PRIMARY KEY, FOREIGN KEY, UNIQUE, CHECK, NOT NULL, DEFAULT) organized by their scope (column-level vs table-level) and showing common enforcement actions like CASCADE, SET NULL, and RESTRICT.
diagrams.purpose: Provides a comprehensive overview of all constraint types available in DBMS and the actions that can be taken when constraint violations occur, helping learners understand when and how to apply each constraint type
